{"step_by_step_thinking": "Cytokeratin is a protein expressed by epithelial cells and is commonly used as an immunohistochemical marker to identify epithelial tumors. Multiple documents in the retrieved sources confirm that cytokeratins are used in the diagnosis of various epithelial malignancies. For example, in sebaceous carcinoma, cytokeratin 5.2 is positive; in squamous cell carcinoma, high-molecular-weight cytokeratins (e.g., CK5/6) are positive; in mammary Paget disease, low molecular weight cytokeratins (e.g., CK7) are expressed; and in Krukenberg tumors, cytokeratin AE1/AE3 is positive, indicating epithelial origin. These findings demonstrate that cytokeratins are used as markers to identify and classify epithelial tumors. Although tumor markers are typically associated with circulating molecules (like CEA or CA-125), cytokeratins function as tissue-based markers in pathology, particularly in immunohistochemistry, to confirm epithelial origin. Therefore, cytokeratin is considered a tumor marker in the context of epithelial tumor diagnosis and classification.", "answer_choice": "A"}
